contradictions.description: Source A omits all specific factual details regarding the election results, such as the election date, popular vote counts for all candidates, electoral vote counts for all candidates, and the breakdown of Lincoln's performance in free and slave states, which are extensively provided in Source B.
contradictions.quote_reference: Source A: 'Abraham Lincoln was elected President during this event.' (Lacks specific details). Source B: 'The election occurred on November 6, 1860. Lincoln received 1,866,452 popular votes and 180 electoral votes. Stephen A. Douglas received 1,375,157 popular votes and 12 electoral votes. John C. Breckinridge received 847,953 popular votes and 72 electoral votes. John Bell received 590,631 popular votes and 39 electoral votes. Lincoln carried all the free states, except in New Jersey where the electoral vote was divided, with him receiving four out of seven. In the fifteen slave states, Lincoln received no electoral vote. In ten states, not one person had voted for Lincoln. Of the 303 electoral votes, Lincoln received 180, while the aggregate of all against him numbered 123, giving him an absolute majority of 57.'

contradictions.description: Source B omits any mention of the pre-election negative circulars that portrayed Lincoln as a 'dangerous radical' and 'enemy of the institutions of the South', which Source A claims were unsettling and influential.

Lincoln was oppressed with the belief that he would not live to see the end of the national crisis.' Source A: (No mention of this personal experience).

The Democratic party had nominated Stephen Douglas, but an extreme slavery faction nominated John C. Breckenridge... A 'Constitutional Union Party' also nominated John Bell of Tennessee... The popular vote was nearly a million (930,170) more against him than for him... Fifteen states gave Lincoln no electoral vote, and in nine states, he received no popular vote. The slave states, or 'the Solid South,' were squarely against Lincoln.'

contradictions.description: The sources contradict on the conditions under which force would be used during the resupply attempt, as stated in Lincoln's notification to Governor Pickens. Source A states that 'if the resupply attempt was not resisted, no effort to introduce men, arms, or ammunition would be made without further notice, or in case of an attack upon the Fort,' implying a conditional and delayed use of military force. Source B, however, claims Lincoln's notice stated the attempt would be made 'peaceably if possible, or by force otherwise,' suggesting an immediate readiness to use force if resistance occurred.
